title: Listening - Part 4: Leaving the City for the Countryside
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people describing their experiences of moving from a large city to a rural village

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d always lived in London, but [21]the noise and pollution were starting to make me feel constantly on edge[/21]. I moved to a tiny village in the Peak District. The silence at night was a bit spooky at first, but [26]the most unexpected benefit has been the dramatic improvement in the quality of my sleep[/26]. I hadn't realized how much the city lights and distant traffic were affecting me. Now I wake up feeling genuinely refreshed. It’s transformed my mood during the day, making me much more patient and productive. || Speaker 2: We moved because [22]we wanted our children to grow up with more space and a closer connection to the natural world[/22]. In the city, they were always indoors. Here, they’re out in the fields every day. However, [27]the biggest challenge has been the total lack of reliable public transport[/27]. You can't just hop on a tube or a bus. If you forget to buy milk, it’s a twenty-minute drive to the nearest shop. It requires a level of forward planning that I still find quite exhausting and frustrating. || Speaker 3: I’m a writer, so I can work anywhere. [23]I chose to move to a remote cottage because I thought the isolation would help my concentration[/23]. It did, but [28]I was surprised by how much I struggled with the feeling of being an outsider in such a tight-knit community[/28]. People are friendly enough, but it takes years to be truly accepted. You’re the 'person from the city' for a long time. I’ve had to make a real effort to join local groups and volunteer at the village hall just to start breaking down those social barriers. || Speaker 4: I was looking for a way to reduce my living costs after my divorce. [24]The main draw of the countryside was the fact that I could get a much larger property for a fraction of the price of my city flat[/24]. It’s been great for my finances. But the practicalities are tough. [29]The most rewarding aspect, though, has been the sense of communal responsibility when things go wrong[/29]. When my pipes froze last winter, three neighbors were at my door with heaters before I’d even finished calling the plumber. You just don't get that kind of immediate, unasked-for help in a big city. || Speaker 5: I’d spent my whole life in the tech industry and [25]I moved to a farm because I wanted to prove to myself that I could survive without a screen in front of my face[/25]. I wanted to be more self-sufficient. It’s been a steep learning curve. [30]I’ve found that the physical labor involved in maintaining a rural property is far more demanding than I ever imagined[/30]. My back is constantly aching and I’m always exhausted, but there’s a deep satisfaction in knowing I grew my own food and fixed my own roof. It’s a much more grounded way to live, even if it is painful.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to move.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions the noise and pollution of London were making her feel on edge.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted more space for the children and a connection to nature.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 thought the isolation would help her focus on her writing.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 moved because she could get a larger property for a lower price.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to prove he could survive without technology and be self-sufficient.

options.A: the possibility of getting more for their money
options.B: a concern for the well-being of their family
options.C: the need to escape a professional failure
options.D: a desire to pursue a specific rural hobby
options.E: the hope of finding a better-paid job
options.F: the negative impact of an urban environment
options.G: the belief that it would improve their work focus
options.H: a personal challenge regarding their lifestyle

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions about their new life.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a dramatic improvement in the quality of her sleep.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions the total lack of reliable public transport as the biggest challenge.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 struggled with being an outsider in a tight-knit community.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 found the sense of communal responsibility and neighborly help rewarding.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 notes that the physical labor is far more demanding than imagined.

options.A: a sense of disappointment with the local amenities
options.B: the difficulty of maintaining distant friendships
options.C: frustration with the local transport infrastructure
options.D: an unexpected improvement in their health
options.E: the pleasure of observing local wildlife
options.F: the surprising amount of physical effort required
options.G: the challenge of being accepted by the locals
options.H: the comfort of a supportive social network
